fuzzy_prd: We need a stateless filtering engine that, given a declarative rule and a batch of incoming blockchain blocks, decides which of those blocks contain transactions we care about. Each block carries a list of transactions; each transaction has an identifier hash, may optionally have a kind (contract deployment, contract method call, or a plain token transfer), and may carry receipt events (token movements, smart-contract log messages). The caller describes what they want with a single predicate — for example 'give me every block where a transaction minted or transferred a specific fungible token', or 'give me deployments from a particular account address', or 'give me any transaction that emitted a log message from a given contract containing a certain substring'. The engine should evaluate the predicate across all provided blocks and report back: whether anything matched at all, how many blocks had at least one matching transaction, and the identifiers of every matched transaction in block-then-in-block order. The report format must be consistent across all predicate types. For the smart-contract log predicate, the contract identifier field and the payload substring both support a wildcard that matches anything. For the deployment predicate with a standard-trait filter, we explicitly do not detect which trait a deployment implements, so that form never selects any transaction. The whole thing should behave like a pure function with no side effects — given the same predicate and the same blocks, always returns the same result.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: main(stdin: JSON object with predicate and blocks) -> stdout exactly: triggered=<true|false>, apply_block_count=<n>, matched_transactions=<comma-separated txids>

trigger_keywords: what are the three output lines
exact format of the predicate result
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The adapter always prints exactly three lines in this order: 'triggered=<true|false>', 'apply_block_count=<n>', 'matched_transactions=<csv>'. The csv is comma-separated txids in block order then in-block order. When nothing matched, matched_transactions= has an empty value after the equals sign. Each line is newline-terminated.

trigger_keywords: what is the wildcard value for print event
how to match any contract in print predicate
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For the print_event predicate: the contract_identifier field accepts the literal string '*' as a wildcard that matches any contract identifier. The contains field also accepts '*' as a wildcard that matches any payload. Both wildcards can be combined. Any other value is an exact-match string.

trigger_keywords: how is the print event payload decoded
how to match the contains substring
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The print event raw_value is a hex-encoded Clarity value. For substring matching the engine decodes it to a human-readable string representation first, then checks whether that string contains the predicate's 'contains' value. For example hex '0x0d00000010616263736f6d652d76616c7565616263' decodes to a string containing 'some-value'.

trigger_keywords: what happens with sip09 or sip10 predicate
trait detection in contract deployment predicate
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The implement_trait form of the contract_deployment predicate (values 'sip09', 'sip10', or '*') never selects any transaction because trait detection is not performed. It always produces triggered=false, apply_block_count=0, matched_transactions=.

trigger_keywords: what is the default transaction kind when omitted
native transfer when kind is absent
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a transaction's 'kind' field is omitted, it defaults to native_token_transfer type. A contract_deployment predicate with 'deployer' form only matches transactions whose kind.type is 'contract_deployment'; it never matches contract_call or native_token_transfer transactions.

Full Chainhook predicate evaluation: FT/NFT/STX asset events, print events, contract deployment matching, contract-call predicates, transaction-id predicates, wildcard principals/assets, receipt/transaction matching, output ordering across blocks and transactions, and stateless evaluation.

Evaluate the predicate against the blocks array and emit only matching events/transactions in deterministic block/transaction/event order. Include contract_call and transaction_id predicate behavior from public_test_cases.

The embedded public_test_cases are part of oracle_data and are authoritative for exact input shapes, action/operation names, stdout line order, whitespace, error categories, and deterministic formatting. When prose and expected_output differ, expected_output is the pass-critical contract.

Contract-call predicates and transaction-id predicates are full first-class features. Contract-call matching must use the PRD/public-case predicate fields for contract identifier and method/function name, and txid matching must select exactly the transaction with the requested id. Include these matches in the standard three-line aggregate report.

Every predicate evaluation outputs exactly three lines in this order: triggered=<true|false>, apply_block_count=<number of selected blocks>, and matched_transactions=<comma-separated matched transaction ids or empty>. Do not output matched_blocks or matched_events. Public test cases are authoritative for this report format.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: behave like a pure function with no side effects
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The engine is stateless: no database, no file I/O, no network. The adapter reads one JSON request from stdin containing both the predicate and the complete blocks array, evaluates, prints three lines, and exits.

Transaction kind type 'native_token_transfer' — the STX (native token) transfer. This is distinct from FT/NFT token events in the receipt. STX events are matched by the stx_event predicate scope, not by transaction kind.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific information about that constraint. Please refer to the project PRD and the golden implementation under hirosystems__chainhook/golden/components/ for the authoritative specification.

feature1_ft_event.json.description: Evaluate a fungible-token event predicate against a sequence of blocks. The predicate names a token asset identifier and a set of accepted actions (mint, transfer, burn). A block is selected when it holds a transaction that emitted a fungible-token event whose action is among the accepted set AND whose asset identifier equals the one named by the predicate. The report states whether any block was selected, how many blocks were selected, and the identifiers of the matched transactions.

feature2_nft_event.json.description: Evaluate a non-fungible-token event predicate against a sequence of blocks. The predicate names a token asset identifier and a set of accepted actions (mint, transfer, burn). A block is selected when it holds a transaction that emitted a non-fungible-token event whose action is among the accepted set AND whose asset identifier equals the one named by the predicate. The report states whether any block was selected, how many blocks were selected, and the identifiers of the matched transactions.

feature3_stx_event.json.description: Evaluate a native-token event predicate against a sequence of blocks. The predicate names a set of accepted actions (mint, transfer, lock, burn) and does not constrain any asset identifier. A block is selected when it holds a transaction that emitted a native-token event whose action is among the accepted set. The report states whether any block was selected, how many blocks were selected, and the identifiers of the matched transactions.

feature4_print_event.json.description: Evaluate a smart-contract print-event predicate against a sequence of blocks. The predicate names a target contract identifier and a substring constraint. Only events published under the print topic are considered. A print event matches when its publishing contract identifier equals the predicate's contract identifier (or the predicate uses the wildcard '*' for the contract identifier) AND the human-readable decoding of the event's payload contains the predicate's substring (or the predicate uses the wildcard '*' for the substring, which matches any payload). A block is selected when it holds a transaction emitting at least one matching print event. The report states whether any block was selected, how many blocks were selected, and the identifiers of the matched transactions.

feature5_contract_deployment.json.description: Evaluate a contract-deployment predicate against a sequence of blocks that may contain both contract-deployment and contract-call transactions. Two predicate forms exist. In the deployer form, a deployment transaction is selected when its deployed contract identifier begins with the named deployer prefix, and the wildcard '*' selects every deployment transaction. In the implemented-trait form (selecting deployments that implement a standard token trait), no transaction is selected because trait detection is not performed. Only deployment transactions can ever be selected by this predicate. The report states whether any block was selected, how many blocks were selected, and the identifiers of the matched transactions.

feature6_contract_call.json.description: Evaluate a contract-call predicate against a sequence of blocks that may contain both contract-call and contract-deployment transactions. The predicate names a target contract identifier and a target method name. A contract-call transaction is selected when both its called contract identifier and its invoked method name equal the values named by the predicate. Only contract-call transactions can be selected. The report states whether any block was selected, how many blocks were selected, and the identifiers of the matched transactions.

feature7_txid.json.description: Evaluate a transaction-identifier predicate against a sequence of blocks. The predicate names an exact transaction identifier hash. A transaction is selected when its identifier hash equals the named value, regardless of the transaction's kind. The report states whether any block was selected, how many blocks were selected, and the identifiers of the matched transactions.
